title: ThamesWater Andrio Emilio application
text:
Preface
Hi!
I’m Andrio, I received a pre-call screening for the performance and risk analyst role here at ThamesWater. I wanted to be proactive and have all my research, preparation and skills in this Quarto book. This is meant to be candid and for my personal use. As such it will show my thought process between each chapter and also my (amazing) personality.

I hope whoever reads this enjoys (including myself)!
:)

title: Past experience
text:
Excel
I have been using excel in the workplace for nearly 10 years now. In my first role I was a data researcher and processor. This involved collecting ONS & local authority data, transforming, cleaning and ultimately providing analysis with visualisations. I primarily used vlookup to join time series data with the next entry. Conditional formatting to track differences and bespoke analysis with pivot charts and pivot tables. This gave me the foundations to transition towards my first analyst role at imperial college London. A hands on role to which I had to give more application and thought towards the processes required to transform and present highly sensitive HR data. Alongside this, I was tasked with more and more data to handle and had to learn automation skills. This led me to developing macros for the use of initial data transformation and soon introduced me to powerBI. Now I use excel sparingly, I have tracker templates to record my response rates for tasks as well as a personal diary. I have also recoreded a systematic review in excel (a database recording features from over 100 academic papers) .


SQL
I have used SQL in tandem with power BI to connect and refresh data for the use of weekly, monthly and annual reports at my role in Network rail. I have completed several modules during my masters study that used SQL. These focused upon creating spatial databases as well as spatial joins. Since, I have completed a 7 week analyst course in which practical application of SQL was used. This involved using SQL to connect to databases, create data, create tables, transform data, merge, join & append.


Power BI
I have numerous years experience in power BI. This started in my role as a hr information insight analyst at imperial college. In this I created bespoke reports based upon foi requests as well as monthly HR necessary data (such as PTO, sickness and diversity metrics). In my role at network rail I used power BI top create asset based metrics for stakeholders decision making. An example of a project from start to finish would be a vegetation risk asset PBI model report. This tracked from live recorded mothly input data of vegetation risk on the railway. It covered species, protected type and seasonality. It also recorded previous intervention & planned removal. These were refreshed with every new report (usually an excel document) that would then be transformed in M-query to handle anomalous values, different data types and wrong inputs. For stakeholders, they would a live map showing new/planned vegetation management and metric charts & tables to show if they are meeting required quotas.

Asset management project you did at network rail to assess performance of track. Go in depth with charts, spatial visualization and automation and how this benefited stakeholders across the company.

title: Past experience
text:
Other technical experience

Python
I have used Python for model creation and analysis of data in my masters studies at UCL. My dissertation project was computed in python and this involved the use of unsupervised/supervised models such as random forest and k means clusters. I have also done some regression analysis and used libraries such as pandas, numpy and scikit learn. Because of this I am used to working in jupyter notebooks and would love the opportunity to pursue this further


Geospatial tools
My geosptaial knowledge started within my role at network rail and led me to pursue a masters at UCL. I completed an Msc in Urban spatial science in which gave me a strong foundation in geospatial analysis and also allowed me to improve my autonomous skills to meet deadline and choose suitable projects. Because of this I have used a vast range of geospatial data and have a knowledge of many areas, including networks, remote sensing and data science.
I used geospatial tools in an applied asset-heavy environment at Network Rail. There, I worked with GIS specialists on mapping over 3000 miles of track for the Southern route, helping turn asset and engineering data into more useful visual reporting. I used tools such as ArcGIS, QGIS, and map-based visualisation approaches, and I also integrated Mapbox visuals into Power BI reports so stakeholders could see issues spatially rather than just in tables. That was especially useful for visualising track-related issues and helping teams understand where problems or risk-related areas were concentrated across the network.


MS access & databricks

no formal application but have planned time on courses starting next week.
Training for access: here
Training for databricks: here

title: Past experience
text:
Asset based environments

BI reports
I’ve worked in an asset-based environment through my role at Network Rail, where the data and reporting were closely tied to physical infrastructure and operational performance. That meant working with engineering and operational datasets linked to the rail network, and helping teams understand where issues, risks, or constraints were appearing across the assets. I think that experience is relevant because it taught me how to use data not just for reporting, but to support decisions in a complex infrastructure setting
One of my main responsibilities at Network Rail was supporting Kent and Sussex track teams through BI reporting. I integrated over 15 data sources into Power BI and used performance measures such as KPIs, speed restrictions, and risk-related reporting to help stakeholders understand how the network was performing. Stakeholders could move away from manual reporting and instead use a more consistent view of performance to prioritise work and track issues over time.

title: Past experience
text:
Maintenance & communication

Maintaining existing data management processes, how have you done this before?

Making sure I understand the process end to end, where data comes from, how its transformed, who will be using and where are the risks.
You have had a Microsoft one note diary recording of your systematic review to track when changes were made and what the change was.
Understanding of existing data management processes by communicating and organising meetings with data teams and stakeholders to understand unfamiliar data
Creating very transparent and available documents for stakeholders and personal use (available in sharepoint) to explain data
Monitoring key data quality metrics in automated BI report that assesses weekly, quarterly and yearly performance against previous entry.

What metrics did you use to present this information? List all the tools, charts and visualisations.

Data quality metrics

missing values / null counts
duplicate records
invalid or out-of-range values
consistency of categories / naming
unexpected period changes
completeness by source or region
refresh success / failed updates
record counts compared with previous periods
percentage change against prior week / quarter / year
exception counts / flagged anomalies

Performance metrics

KPI movement over time
period-on-period comparisons
count of issues / restrictions / events
trend direction
thresholds breached
concentration by area / route / asset group
backlog / open vs resolved items
performance against target


List example of collaboration

Network rail vegetation BI report, how you used an iterative continuous improvement process

title: Past experience
text:
Producing detailed reports
I’ve produced detailed reports in both professional and academic settings. I’m comfortable adapting the level of detail depending on the audience. At Network Rail, that involved developing reports and presentations based on engineering and operational data. As the aim was to make complex information clear enough for stakeholders to act on. It was important to present the detail accurately while still making the key messages easy to understand.
During my master’s, I also worked on group projects where we had to take technical analysis and present it in a structured and professional way. That helped me build confidence in explaining analytical work clearly. In my dissertation, I worked closely with industry experts to shape the focus of the project and make sure the outputs were relevant as well as technically robust. That experience taught me that producing a strong report is not just about analysis it’s also about understanding the audience, communicating clearly, and making the findings useful.

title: Research
text:
Company overview
“Every day, we serve 16 million customers across London and the Thames Valley. From that first cup of tea in the morning through to baths at bedtime.
As well as supplying water to your taps, we take wastewater away. We collect and treat sewage, returning clean water to our rivers.
Delivering life’s essential service, so our customers, communities and environment can thrive. We’re here to keep taps flowing for generations to come. Find out more about how we’re upgrading our network.”
It is a very informative site, compiling pollution reduction, environmental information, five year plans and assessing performance.

title: Research
text:
Open data strategy, so data is freely available for everyone to access.
PR24 five year plan 2025 - 2030. Involving strategy, cost efficiency, expected outcome and data models.
Biggest upgrade in 150 years with 20 planned investment focused on reducing pollution protecting water quality and installing over 1 million more smart meters.
Digital pages talk about building an enterprise data platform on Databricks Lakehouse

title: Research
text:
Team focus
Why is an asset performance analyst essential?
“Asset performance metrics form the foundation of data-driven maintenance, providing continuous visibility into equipment reliability, maintenance efficiency, and operational trends that would otherwise remain hidden.
Tracking the right metrics connects maintenance activities to business outcomes, helping teams reduce unplanned downtime, control costs, extend equipment life, and demonstrate measurable value to leadership.
Advanced software platforms automate metric collection and analysis, replacing manual spreadsheets with real-time dashboards, AI-powered insights, and integrated workflows that turn data into action.”
Assessing performance and behaviour, what does this mean for Thames Water?
Performance is about how well the asset is doing
Behaviour is about the pattern of how the asset acts

For performance:

how well is it doing?
is it meeting expected standards?
how many incidents / failures / downtime events are there?

For behavioural:

what pattern does it show?
when does it tend to go wrong?
under what conditions does it behave differently?
is there a recurring operating signature that suggests risk?


Why this is important

title: Research
text:
Policy, ethics and competences

Take care



Passionate about everything we do



Be respectful and value everyone



Reach higher, be better



Take ownership



Be proud, Be blue / Team Thames

title: Preparation
text:
1 Can you talk me through your background?
I started working in data during my undergraduate degree. In my first year I scored 100% in a business statistics module and this led me to change my degree to analytics. I then sought experience in a Leeds based startup alongside my degree during the second and 3rd year. In this role I was a data researcher and processor. This meant collecting, cleaning, transforming and creating charts for councils using ONS and local authority data. Because of this experience in excel I started my first graduate role and imperial college london. Wherein in the first week my manager gave me a huge power BI book, said learn as much as you can and by the end of the second week I want to see a report from you. This led me to learn power BI and has been a staple for my work. It was especially important as i started my role at network rail, learning asset and engineering data, how to visualise these metrics and understand what data insights stakeholders want to see! With this I got the oportunity to work alongside a lead geospatial scientist, and led to a power BI project of mapping asset faults spatially. This introduced me to geospatial analysis in which I studied a masters in at UCL and scored a distinction.


2 Why are you interested in this role?
Im interested in the role as it has both familiarity in working within an asset data based environment but also the opportunity to learn how to apply the skills I have learnt and be a part of a winder data team to work for real world data problems to which I can see a clear benefit. I think in the past, especially during my masters degree, I learnt that working alongside stakeholders and experts who wanted to know more about the data in an industry they have expertise in knowing I could do just that. I also think this is a position where I can develop my analytical skills further and keep learning, especially within databricks.
I’m also drawn to the scale and complexity of the organisation. It’s the kind of environment where you have to think carefully, work collaboratively, and make decisions that are both practical and responsible. That really appeals to me, because I enjoy roles where I can contribute in a structured way, take ownership of my work, and help improve how things are done.


3 What attracted you to a role in asset operations / wastewater / utilities?
The basis for my application is my prior skills in an asset based environment at network rail. Since this role I have explored other areas within data, including geospatial analysis and research intensive roles. Now I feel that my skills in insight, visualisation and communicating this is well geared toward a role at Thames Water
More info **


4 Multiple sources to create a clear performance report
S
At network rail I worked as the main data analyst for the kent and sussex track team. In this role I had full autonomy on how I present routine reporting as well as adhoc analysis. Because I was known in my teams it often led to my skills being offered to other teams too. This was the case in Vegetation risk for planning and vegetation team.
T
For this the vegetation team had asked me to help them use their data of problematic vegetation on track with already planned maintenance data that could be extracted from data streams already.
A
For this I integrated the vegetation data that was weekly manually updated by asking the vegetation team to place the new spreadsheets in a sharepoint folder. This allowed me to automate the processes. Once I created the data stream, with the already established planning stream my next task was to find a unique identifier, so we could see if any planning work had been allocated to nearby vegetation problem sites. This was a difficult task as the vegetation data was manually input and thinking about joining this with other sources of data hadn’t been thought of. Although what was in this data set was geospatial data. Using this I was able to map the whole kent and sussex track, use the actual identifying informationn of the planned data stream as well as vegetation geospatial data to clearly display areas that are in already bound maintenance.
R
The output was a weekly scheduled automated traffic light key indicated map in which displayed areas that had planned maintenance and those that didnt. This helped both the planning and vegetation team to create a clear plan, reduce time wasted in already maintained sites and reduced costs in double booking.


5 How strong are you in SQL?
I have experience, creating, querying and transforming data in SQL. In the past I have used SQL to spatially join, extract data for power BI usage and experiment. I have developed a small project on this page to show my basic understanding of SQL. I have also undergone refresher courses to make sure I have the baseline for success in this role.


6 About a time youve explained complex analysis to non-technical stakeholders.
S My msc dissertation project was very ambitious but it stemmed from one main purpose. How can I use data to help a wider audience? My project aimed at using novel data at local London homeless charities to help them make more appropriate decisions in finding and then better interventions. The analysis combined geospatial data, computer vision and predictive modelling but the intended audience was charity outreach workers rather than technical analysts.
T
I needed to make sure my findings were useful, understandable and could be easily interpreted, while still being academically robust and keeping track of the sensitive nature of the data.
A
I collobarated with ThamesReach which supplied me with sensitive geospatial data from streetlink. I divided my approach in two ways, academically and for presentation purpose. I built a python based methodology using geospatial analysis and computer vision to understand how built environment features related to rough sleeper movement and displacement. When presenting my findings, I used example images gathered from my computer vision model to present findings and avoided going in detail about the intricacies in building the model. Rather focusing on what these results meant in practice for outreach workers and how this could support understanding of the environments people moved through.
R
The project achieved a distinction grade and more importantly gave the charity a clear understanding of displacement patterns in a way that was relevant to their work on the ground.


7 How have you used Power BI in previous roles?
STAR Example
Situation
In my role at network rail, my bi reports had gained traction. A colleague from the vegetation team wanted some monthly reported visuals to be produced without the need for him to chase data.
Task
My task was a start to finish BI report for the vegetation team. I had to understand the data, how and where the data is ussually stored and what the data insights are used for in the team. One of the main goals was for me to give the BI report to the vegetation team where they could understand how to use the visuals with the familiarity of previous insights.
Action
This led me to an iterative process. To understand the data I asked the colleague what insights he usually extracts and what he would further like this to be. I also showed him previous BI reports and asked him what visuals he tended to like or understood. We both agreed that charts and visuals that show the insights explicitly were great, and we adapted some simple charts further on with instruction pages and tooltips to aid the end user. He also found mapping vegetation issues to be life changing, he then recognised where on track issues were and this reminded him as to reasons why (i.e. part of the track being narrow orforested area. Another part of this process was his manual input of data, he found this frustrating and time consuming. I understand that the data was recorded in excel and stored in sharepoint. With power bi I showed him that I could automatically do his time consuming transformations and data cleaning.
Result
The result was a fully automated power BI report that had clear simple visuals that my colleague could use to show the performance of vegetation removal efforts. It also allowed him more free time to allocate on tasks.


8 Maintained data quality?
S At regenerate outcomes I worked on agricultural and soil carbon modelling, where the quality of a farmer submitted logbook directly affected model inputs and carbon credit estimation.
T
To review and validate information coming from over 100 farms, identify anomalies and make sure the data was accurate enough to be used in modelling.
A
I reviewed farmer logbooks from template stage through to modelling input and transformed the data into usable outputs in R Studio. I checked for anomalies, validated entries, and worked directly with farmers to resolve unclear or inconsistent inputs. I also carried out routine quality assurance of geospatial reporting using visualisations and assurance metrics, so issues could be spotted early rather than after they had fed into the model.
R
This improved the reliability of the modelling inputs and meant the data going into soil carbon and credit estimation work was far more robust and usable. It also reduced ambiguity by improving communication with non-technical stakeholders supplying the source information.


9 Tell me about a time you turned complex analysis into an actionable recommendation.

Working with Thames reach for dissertation

S
In my dissertation, I was investigating how built-environment features and perceptions of safety were linked to rough sleeping patterns in London. The project involved combining person-level referral data with street-level image data and spatial information, which made the analysis quite complex. The challenge was that there were lots of possible variables and patterns, the end goal was to generate insights that could actually be useful for policy and intervention with charities such as thamesreach.
T
My task was to analyse this complex, multi-source dataset and identify which environmental factors were most strongly associated with rough sleeping locations, then translate that into recommendations that would be understandable and relevant for a non-technical audience at Thames Reach
A
To do that, I brought together multiple datasets, including geospatial referral data and street-level image-based measures of the urban environment. I used methods like clustering to identify different types of rough sleeping locations, spatial autocorrelation measures such as Moran’s I to understand patterns in the data, and random forest models to explore which built-environment and perceived-safety variables were most predictive.
Because the outputs were quite technical and there were ethical sensitivities around oversimplifying homelessness. I focused on identifying the main patterns, checking that the findings were robust, and then translating them into clear messages about which environmental characteristics were associated with higher concentrations of rough sleeping and assessing this throughout current policy.
R
The outcome was that I was able to turn a complex and highly multidimensional analysis into a practical recommendation: that interventions should be more spatially targeted, and that local authorities and support services could use environmental and perceptual indicators to help prioritise areas for outreach and prevention.
I scored a distinction. (yay)


10 When your analysis supported decision making?
S At Regenerate Outcomes, part of my role involved both reviewing evidence and turning analytical findings into something management could use when developing modelling approaches.
T
I needed to help support decision-making around soil carbon model development using both practical data analysis and evidence from research.
A
I reviewed over 150 academic papers as part of a systematic review to identify relevant features for model development. Alongside that, I worked on farmer logbook analysis and geospatial QA in R Studio, which helped test how usable and reliable different inputs were in practice. I then synthesised these findings and presented them to management in a way that linked evidence, data quality, and model development choices.
R
My work helped management make better-informed decisions on model development by grounding discussion in both evidence and operational data quality. It also showed I could move from detailed analysis to practical recommendation.


11 How do you handle situations where stakeholders want different things from the data?
S
T
A
R


12 Use of financial data
S
Evaluating the prfoability of bio fertilisers v synthentic. You want to clarify the project scope and criteria for analysis. This means you need to engage with stakeholders to clarify desired outputs. Have data arranged in a manner where you can apply analysis
T
Use the information and benchmark with industry standards/ stakeholders in order to compute predictive carbon credits.
A
Looking for time period, revenue sources and costs. Needs to be easily explainable, so note your calculations, data transformations and sources. Compile in a one note file and show analysis through R studio. Suggest implementation of power BI for presenting purposes. By working iteratively with stakeholders, it was suggested that using power BI and explaining the calculations on BI pages for transparency was ideal.
R
They could see the direct outputs and costs of synthetic fertiliser, the time it took for the fertilisers to use and then the prospective credits earned.


13 Handled sensitive data
Imperial college london HR data, FOI requests race requests


14 Have you worked with operational / asset / infrastructure data before?


15 Resolve conflicting views on data
S
At Network Rail, I was the main data analyst supporting the Kent and Sussex track teams. The reporting drew from multiple internal systems and Oracle, and different stakeholders often had different views on which metrics mattered most or how performance should be interpreted.
T
I needed to gather the right information, create a consistent reporting approach, and make sure the final outputs worked for both operational teams and senior stakeholders.
A
I spent time working directly with the different teams to understand what each group needed from the reporting and where the differences were. Instead of treating it as a technical issue only, I used those conversations to align definitions and priorities before building them into the Power BI dashboard. I also kept the dashboard iterative, so stakeholder feedback could be incorporated over time rather than locking it down too early. Because I was integrating data from many sources, I could also check where disagreements were really about interpretation and where they came from inconsistent data inputs.
R
The end result was a bespoke dashboard used by 25+ senior stakeholders, with clearer reporting and better shared understanding across teams. It improved confidence in the outputs because people could see their needs had been considered and the metrics had been shaped collaboratively


16 Tell me about a time you identified a risk or performance issue from data.


17 What kinds of statistical modelling have you done?
My experience ranges from practical operational analysis to more formal modelling. In academic and research settings, I’ve used Python-based modelling, geospatial analysis and machine learning approaches to identify patterns and relationships in data. In more operational roles, statistical modelling has been more about trend analysis, KPI monitoring, anomaly detection, and identifying where performance patterns may indicate risk or require further investigation.


18 The role mentions statistical modelling. What does that mean to you in practice?

Statistical modelling in practice is about using data in a structured way to understand performance, identify patterns, and support decisions. It can include more advanced predictive techniques, but in many operational roles it also means applying methods like trend analysis, KPI monitoring, and anomaly detection. Trend analysis is about looking at how a metric changes over time to understand whether performance is stable, improving, or deteriorating. KPI monitoring is about tracking the most important measures against targets, thresholds, or expected ranges so it is clear where performance is on track and where it may need attention. Anomaly detection is about spotting values or patterns that fall outside what would normally be expected, which can help flag possible operational issues, emerging risk, or data quality problems. So overall, I’d describe statistical modelling as using data systematically to move beyond simple reporting and help identify where further investigation or action may be needed.


19 Can you explain a time when you had to analyse a complex set of data and make recommendations based on your findings?
S
For my MSc dissertation at UCL, I focused on homeless rough sleepers and displacement across London boroughs. The project involved working with sensitive geospatial referral data gathered through collaboration with Thames Reach and the StreetLink application. It was a complex problem because I wasn’t just analysing one dataset I was trying to understand a real-world social issue by combining spatial data, environmental context, and policy considerations around homelessness and displacement
T
My task was to build a robust analytical approach that could identify patterns in rough sleeper movement and the environments associated with displacement, while making sure the work was ethically sound, anonymised, and useful for stakeholders. I also needed to go beyond pure modelling and think about the policy relevance of the findings, in other words, what the results meant for homelessness outreach and decision-making.
A
I started by carefully preparing and structuring the referral and geospatial data, making sure anonymity and data security were maintained throughout. I then used Python, including tools such as pandas, numpy and scikit-learn, to build analytical models that combined geospatial analysis with computer vision methods. The aim was to link features of the built environment to possible migration or displacement patterns among rough sleepers.
Alongside the modelling, I interpreted the findings in a wider policy context. I looked at what the patterns might suggest about displacement, how environments may influence movement, and how those insights could support outreach services. So it wasn’t just a technical exercise it was also about translating the outputs into something meaningful and responsible for real-world use.
I then presented the findings clearly to homeless outreach workers, focusing on the practical implications and making sure the recommendations were understandable to non-technical stakeholders.
R
The result was a robust and novel model that helped predict the flow of rough sleepers in cases of displacement, while maintaining strong data anonymity and security. More importantly, the work gave outreach workers a better understanding of the kinds of environments rough sleepers may migrate through when displaced, which made the findings practically useful as well as analytically strong. I think that project showed that I can handle complex data, build models, and also connect the findings back to policy and stakeholder needs.


20. How would you handle disagreements with management about your analysis?
This has happened in the past, especially between stakeholders with expert knowledge. An example of this is when creating BI reports for network rail. I found that having clear communication of what insight the data should show is imperative to create insights.


21 What methods do you use to ensure data accuracy?
Before analysing any data you must ensure it’s cleaned up by removing any duplicates, correcting any errors, and dealing with any missing data. Use various data validation techniques to ensure the data’s integrity. For me, it’s better to take time to ensure the data is accurate rather than rush and make decisions based on inaccurate data.
S
T
A
R


22 How do you ensure that your analytics reports are easily understood by non-analytic professionals?
When preparing reports, focus on simplicity and clarity. Use visual aids such as charts and graphs to present data, as they are easier to understand. I also avoid jargon and explain all technical terms. Above all, I ensure that the key findings and recommendations are clearly highlighted.


23 How do you ensure that your analytics reports are easily understood by non-analytic professionals?
“Therefore, when preparing my reports, I focus on simplicity and clarity. I use visual aids such as charts and graphs to present data, as they are easier to understand. I also avoid jargon and explain all technical terms. Above all, I ensure that the key findings and recommendations are clearly highlighted.”


24 Managing competing deadlines


25 How do you handle large data sets?
“first use data wrangling techniques to clean and organise the data. I then use tools such as SQL for data extraction and Python for data analysis. These tools have been effective in managing large data sets and ensuring accurate analysis.”


26 How to react in stressful situations?
S Group project where I was the only confident english speaker, but everyone had to partake in presenting.


27 Give an example of when you had to explain performance trends, risks, or KPIs and make recommendations?


28 What techniques do you use in qualitative data analysis?
The main techniques I use are thematic analysis, reviewing stakeholder feedback for repeated issues or patterns, comparing views across different groups, and summarising unstructured information into clear themes. I also use qualitative insight to support quantitative analysis, for example to understand why a trend may be happening rather than just reporting that it exists. I think qualitative analysis is most useful when it helps connect the numbers to real operational behaviour, stakeholder priorities, or data collection issues. My focus is usually on being structured and consistent, so that the findings are still evidence-based rather than just anecdotal.


29 How do you prioritize your analysis when dealing with multiple projects?
I usually prioritise based on business impact, urgency, and dependency. First, I look at which pieces of work are most critical to operational decisions or external reporting deadlines. Then I consider whether one task is blocking another, for example if a dataset needs cleaning before reporting can happen. I also try to distinguish between work that is urgent and work that is important, because they are not always the same. In practice, I like to break work into clear stages, such as data collection, cleaning, analysis, validation, and output, so I can track progress and adjust if priorities shift. Communication is also important. If I am working across multiple requests, I make sure stakeholders understand timelines and any trade-offs, so expectations stay realistic while quality remains high


30 Where do you see yourself in 5 years?


31 How do you validate the reliability and consistency of your data?
“Ensuring the reliability and consistency of data is crucial. I use various data validation techniques, such as cross-validation and consistency checks. I also regularly update the data to ensure it reflects the current situation. These methods have helped me to provide accurate and reliable data analysis.”


32 How have you monitored data quality metrics in reporting?“
I’ve approached that by building reporting that does not only show performance outcomes, but also helps assess whether the data and metrics are behaving as expected over time. For example, I’d look at consistency across weekly, quarterly, and yearly reporting periods, compare current entries against previous ones, and flag unexpected changes, missing values, unusual spikes, or inconsistencies. The purpose is to make it easier to distinguish between a genuine operational change and something that may be a data issue.


33 What have you done to keep data processes organised and reliable?“
I usually approach that in three parts. First, I build understanding by speaking to the people closest to the data, especially when a dataset or process is unfamiliar. That helps me understand the source, logic, and any known issues. Second, I document things clearly, both for myself and for others, so there’s transparency around definitions, changes, and assumptions. For example, I’ve kept structured change records in OneNote and created clear reference documents for stakeholders in shared spaces like SharePoint. Third, I monitor the outputs through reporting and data quality checks, so if something changes or degrades, it can be picked up early.


34 Explain your employment gap / why did you leave your last role


35 Explain how your skills fit Thames Water beliefs and values
Take care For me, this means being careful, responsible, and consistent in the way I handle data and reporting. In analytical roles, that means making sure data is accurate, checking quality properly, and understanding that poor reporting can affect operational decisions. I take that seriously, especially in environments where services and infrastructure directly affect customers and communities.
Passionate about everything we do I think one of my strengths is that I enjoy getting into the detail of a problem and improving how things work. Whether that is building reporting, cleaning difficult data, or improving a process, I genuinely like turning complexity into something useful. That kind of energy is important in analytical roles because good reporting usually comes from someone who wants to understand the problem properly rather than just produce an output.
Be respectful and value everyone A big part of my experience has involved working with non-technical stakeholders and translating technical analysis into something clear and practical. I think being a good analyst is not just about technical skill, it is also about listening properly, understanding other people's priorities, and making sure people feel comfortable engaging with data even if they are not technical themselves.
Reach higher, be better I am someone who is always trying to improve how I work. That could mean learning new tools, refining how I validate data, improving a dashboard, or finding a better way to explain analysis. I like roles where there is room to keep learning and raising the standard of the work, and that is one of the reasons Thames Water appeals to me.
Take ownership This value fits me especially well. I like taking responsibility for my work from start to finish, whether that is gathering data, validating it, analysing it, and then presenting it clearly. I do not like passing problems on without trying to solve them. In reporting roles especially, I think ownership matters because people need to trust that what you produce is accurate, thought through, and useful.
Be proud, be blue What I take from this is having pride in contributing to something important. Thames Water is providing an essential public service, so even analytical work in the background has a real impact. I would take pride in using my skills to support better decisions in a business that affects millions of customers, communities, and the environment.


36 How familiar are you to bio resources and asset management?
My understanding is that bioresources refers to the treatment and management of sludge and related by-products from wastewater treatment, including how they are processed, moved, recycled, and used for value such as energy generation or land application. I would not claim to come from a bioresources background directly, but I am very comfortable learning complex operational areas quickly and translating them into performance reporting and analysis. In terms of asset management, I do have relevant experience because I have worked in infrastructure-focused environments where understanding physical assets, performance trends, and operational risks was important. What interests me about this role is applying my analytical skills in a utility setting where asset performance and operational efficiency really matter


37 How do you handle large data sets?
Show your proficiency with managing and analyzing large data sets in your response. Discuss the tools and techniques you use to deal with large volumes of data.

When dealing with large data sets, I first use data wrangling techniques to clean and organize the data. I then use tools such as SQL for data extraction and Python for data analysis. These tools have been effective in managing large data sets and ensuring accurate analysis



38 Quantitative analysis
a good practical example is my work at Network Rail. I used quantitative analysis to combine data from more than 15 sources and turn it into reporting on speed restrictions, risk sites, KPIs, and planning information.
The quantitative side involved cleaning and transforming the data, joining sources together, checking consistency across systems, and then analysing performance trends over time. I used SQL for querying and combining datasets, and Power BI to build dashboards that showed trend-based and comparative analysis.
In terms of calculations, that included KPI tracking, period-on-period comparisons, identifying changes in performance, and highlighting areas of higher operational risk. I also used Python scripts to help maintain KPI reporting and automate parts of the process.
For visualisation, I used dashboard visuals in Power BI, including trend charts, summary KPI cards, and geospatial mapping. The maps were especially useful because they helped stakeholders understand where issues were occurring across the route, while the charts and KPI visuals gave a clearer picture of overall performance and change over time.
So that is how I have used quantitative analysis in practice: combining structured data, applying analytical logic and comparisons, and presenting the results in dashboards and maps so stakeholders could make decisions

title: Power BI
text:

Show how you connect to SQL and excel and how you use M-query to transform data before using DAX. Set text rules to provide contextual information of data ie ‘this week _ location has under performed in comparison to all other locations’
interpreting asset and operational data to identify performance trends and risk insights, and sharing findings using clear visualisations and commentary
